text: The video shows the initial view of the large paraesophageal hernia. The surgeon performs a line of intercostal blocks using a green needle, injecting bupivacaine with adrenaline. The Articentio Maryland instrument is introduced to begin taking down adhesions.

The surgeon uses a bipolar wristed instrument to begin the thymectomy, carefully removing fat from the lower portion of the sternum and diaphragm. The instrument is positioned through a port in the third intercostal space, utilizing bipolar energy for precise tissue dissection.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to gently dissect and remove tissue from the diaphragm, revealing the diaphragm muscles. The left-sided instrument retracts while the right-sided instrument identifies the plane, lifting the right inferior pole off the diaphragm. The pericardium is visible, and the surgeon uses the Maryland instrument to peel tissue off the pericardium.

The surgeon uses VATS wristed instruments to reach the left inferior pole of the thymus, maneuvering across to the opposite side with ease. The bipolar instrument is used to dissect up the pleura, angling at right angles for precise tissue manipulation.

The surgeon opens the opposite pleura and proceeds to remove the thymus from the pericardium on the right side. The surgical field reveals significant adhesions, including some between the lung and the phrenic nerve. The surgeon carefully navigates towards the right phrenic nerve, pointing out its location amidst the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon works along the superior vena cava (SVC) using a bipolar instrument, leaving a margin around the thymus. There is visible bleeding from the thymus, likely due to vessels from the inferior pole, lateral reamer vein, and artery.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to manage bleeding and proceeds to dissect tissue from the back of the sternum, following the reamer vein. The thymus is seen flopping over the superior vena cava (SVC), complicating the identification of the innominate vein. The surgeon finishes dissecting the opposite inferior pole using wristed instruments, which allow for precise manipulation and access to deeper areas.
The surgeon uses wristed instruments to peel tissue off the pericardium and continues to remove tissue from the back of the sternum.

The surgeon uses a subxiphoid temoport to obtain a view of the left phrenic nerve, which is obscured by deep fat and a large parasophageal hernia pushing the heart upwards. The lemur artery and vein are visible at the top of the screen, serving as landmarks for locating the innominate vein.

The surgeon advances into the neck region, following the lemur vein to the left superior pole of the thymus. Using a ligature with a hook attachment, the surgeon identifies the thymic vein branching from the innominate vein. The right superior pole of the thymus is retracted with a wristed instrument, and the thymic vein is carefully dissected off the innominate vein.

The surgeon follows the innominate vein across to the lemur vein, removing thymus tissue from beneath the innominate vein using wristed instruments. The left superior pole is visualized, and the surgeon uses a sub-xiphoid camera to assist in the procedure. A specimen bag is introduced through the sub-xiphoid port.

The surgeon uses 8mm robotic ports in the right chest and a 10mm sub-xiphoid port to remove a type A thymoma. The final result is shown, with a 20 French drain placed. Both lungs are inflated and checked for leaks by bubbling under water for ten minutes before removing the drain.
